encounter.reason: Anaphylaxis due to accidental peanut ingestion
encounter.location: WakeMed Raleigh Campus, 3000 New Bern Avenue, Raleigh, NC 27610

labs.name: serum tryptase
labs.note: Confirms mast cell degranulation consistent with anaphylaxis

conditions.name: anaphylaxis due to peanut ingestion
conditions.note: First known allergic reaction and first known peanut exposure. Three-system involvement: cutaneous (urticaria, angioedema), respiratory (wheezing, desaturation), cardiovascular (tachycardia, borderline hypotension).

medications.name: epinephrine 0.15 mg intramuscular injection (EpiPen Jr)
medications.sig: 0.15 mg IM once to the right anterolateral thigh

medications.name: diphenhydramine 15 mg intravenous
medications.reason: anaphylaxis due to peanut ingestion (adjunctive antihistamine therapy)

medications.name: methylprednisolone 12 mg intravenous
medications.reason: anaphylaxis due to peanut ingestion (adjunctive corticosteroid therapy)

medications.name: epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen Jr 0.15 mg), two-pack
medications.sig: Administer 0.15 mg intramuscularly for emergency use in anaphylaxis; carry at all times
medications.reason: peanut allergy with history of anaphylaxis

medications.name: prednisolone 12 mg oral, daily for 5 days
medications.sig: 12 mg (1 mg/kg/day) by mouth once daily for 5 days
medications.reason: anaphylaxis due to peanut ingestion (post-anaphylaxis corticosteroid taper)

medications.name: diphenhydramine 6.25 mg oral, every 6 hours as needed for 48 hours
medications.sig: 6.25 mg by mouth every 6 hours as needed for residual urticaria, for 48 hours

document_references.description: Comprehensive ED physician note by Dr. Trammell documenting the evaluation and management of a 2-year-7-month-old female presenting with anaphylaxis following accidental peanut ingestion at a family gathering. Includes HPI, physical exam findings (urticaria, angioedema, wheezing), ED course with serial vitals and medication administration, lab results (elevated tryptase, CBC), medical decision making, assessment of four diagnoses, and discharge plan with medications, strict avoidance instructions, and allergist referral.

Triage nursing assessment documenting arrival by EMS, ESI Level 2 acuity, initial vital signs, chief complaint of allergic reaction after peanut ingestion, and presenting symptoms including diffuse urticaria, facial angioedema, and wheezing.

Nursing documentation of serial vital signs at six time points over 6-hour observation period, medication administration times and routes for epinephrine, diphenhydramine, methylprednisolone, and albuterol, and clinical status observations documenting progressive symptom resolution.

Discharge documentation including discharge vital signs, discharge medications (EpiPen Jr, prednisolone, diphenhydramine), strict peanut avoidance instructions, return precautions, epinephrine auto-injector training confirmation for both parents, and follow-up instructions including pediatrician visit within 3–5 days and pediatric allergist referral.

Patient/family education materials on proper technique for epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen Jr) administration, including when to use, injection technique, and when to seek emergency care. Both parents received hands-on training and demonstrated correct technique at bedside.

diagnostic_reports.description: BMP was reported as within normal limits. No individual component values were provided in the narrative.

allergies.reaction: anaphylaxis — manifestations include generalized urticaria, facial angioedema (periorbital and lip swelling), bronchospasm with wheezing, tachycardia, and borderline hypotension
allergies.note: First known allergic reaction and first known peanut exposure.
